title: Roast Parsnips
subtitle: Honey-glazed, caramelised at the edges, a sweet companion to the Sunday roast
excerpt: Parsnips roast well but benefit from a coating of honey or maple syrup in the final 10 minutes: the natural sugars caramelise into a sticky, slightly bitter glaze that balances the parsnip's sweetness. Par-boil them first for 5 minutes to guarantee a tender interior before the edges have time to burn.
sourceNotes: Traditional British Sunday roast accompaniment. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: parsnips baked alongside the joint. Honey glaze is a modern adaptation of the classic roasted parsnip method.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Par-boil the parsnips up to 2 hours ahead and set aside. Finish in the oven for the final 40 minutes of the roast.

glossaryTerms.definition: The browning of natural sugars in vegetables through high dry heat, producing complex flavour compounds and a golden-brown surface. Honey or syrup accelerates this on parsnips by increasing the sugar concentration on the surface.

content.text: Parsnips contain more sugar than almost any other root vegetable and

quickly in a hot oven. Par-boiling before roasting ensures the centre is soft before the edges have time to catch; without it, a large parsnip will have a crunchy, almost raw centre while the outside is dark. Honey in the final minutes adds a sticky lacquer and a slightly bitter top note.

items.prepNote: peeled, halved lengthways, large ones quartered

content.text: Bring a large saucepan of salted water to the boil. Add the parsnip pieces and cook for 5 minutes until slightly tender at the edges. Drain and leave to steam-dry in the colander for 2 minutes.

Heat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan). Toss the parsnips in {{olive-oil}} of olive oil and season with {{sea-salt-flakes}} and {{black-pepper}}. Spread in a single layer in a roasting pan. Roast for 35 to 40 minutes, turning once at 20 minutes, until golden-brown on the cut faces.

Drizzle {{honey}} of honey over the parsnips and toss to coat. Return to the oven for a final 8 to 10 minutes until the honey has caramelised and the parsnips are sticky and deeply golden. Watch closely: honey burns faster than the parsnip will further brown.

rows.symptom: Parsnips burning at the thin ends
rows.cause: Thin ends cook faster than the thick body
rows.fix: Cut parsnips into even pieces. Thin ends can be removed from the pan 10 minutes before the body is done.

rows.cause: Honey added too early or oven too hot
rows.fix: Add honey only in the final 8 to 10 minutes, not before. Reduce oven temperature slightly if it catches.

content.text: Parsnips were one of the most important root vegetables in the British diet before potatoes arrived from the Americas in the sixteenth century. They fell to second rank once the potato established itself but remained a fixture on the Sunday roast plate. The honey-glazed version is a twentieth-century elaboration; the older method was simply roasting alongside the joint in its dripping.
